cards.front: Extended ACL number ranges and what they match on.
cards.back: 100-199 (and 2000-2699). Match on: source IP, dest IP, protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP/IP), source port, dest port, TCP flags (`established`), DSCP. Best practice: place close to the SOURCE — kills unwanted traffic before it consumes bandwidth.

cards.front: Extended ACL syntax — deny TCP from 10.1.1.0/24 to host 192.168.1.10 port 23 (Telnet), permit everything else.
cards.back:
```
access-list 100 deny tcp 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.10 eq 23
access-list 100 permit ip any any
interface gi0/0
 ip access-group 100 in
```

cards.front: Named ACL syntax — convert the previous numbered ACL to named.
cards.back:
```
ip access-list extended BLOCK-TELNET
 deny tcp 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.1.10 eq 23
 permit ip any any
interface gi0/0
 ip access-group BLOCK-TELNET in
```
Named ACLs let you EDIT individual lines (sequence numbers) — numbered ACLs don't.

cards.front: How do you insert a line in the middle of a named ACL?
cards.back: Each entry has a sequence number (10, 20, 30 by default). Enter the ACL config and just type with a custom sequence: `ip access-list extended NAME` then `15 permit tcp ...` — inserts between 10 and 20. Resequence with `ip access-list resequence NAME 10 10`.

`eq 80` (port 80). `gt 1023` (greater than 1023). `lt 1024` (less than 1024). `range 1024 65535` (inclusive range). `neq 80` (not equal to 80). Used after a host/network in extended ACL: `permit tcp any any eq 443`.

cards.front: What does the `established` keyword in extended ACL do?
cards.back: Matches TCP segments with ACK or RST flag set — i.e., return traffic for an existing connection. Used to allow OUTBOUND-initiated sessions to receive replies, while still blocking INBOUND-initiated TCP. Example: `permit tcp any any established`.

cards.front: Where should you APPLY a standard vs extended ACL, and why?
cards.back: Standard: close to DESTINATION (matches only on source — placing too far from destination would block legitimate traffic to other destinations from that source). Extended: close to SOURCE (matches on full 5-tuple — drop unwanted packets early to save bandwidth).

cards.front: How do you delete or resequence ACL entries?
cards.back: DELETE: in named-ACL config mode (`ip access-list extended NAME`), use `no <seq>` (e.g., `no 30`) — you cannot delete individual entries from global config. RESEQUENCE: `ip access-list resequence <acl-id> <starting-seq> <increment>` (e.g., `... resequence 100 10 10` renumbers entries 10, 20, 30...).

cards.front: View which ACLs are applied to which interfaces?
cards.back: `show ip interface <name>` — shows inbound + outbound IPv4 ACLs and direction. `show running-config | section interface` also reveals it. `show access-lists` shows the ACL bodies + per-line hit counts but NOT placement.

cards.front: Common IP Protocol numbers (used in ACLs and IP header): ICMP, TCP, UDP, EIGRP, OSPF.
cards.back: ICMP = 1. TCP = 6. UDP = 17. GRE = 47. ESP = 50. AH = 51. EIGRP = 88. OSPF = 89. VRRP = 112. (Same numbers used in IPv4 'Protocol' field and in extended ACL `protocol` field.)
